I don’t post like this often, but after a number of great conversations recently, I’m now actively exploring my next opportunity.

Over the past 12+ years, I’ve partnered with founders and senior leadership teams to scale high-growth SaaS, telecom, fintech, and AI businesses globally. My focus has always been simple: align hiring with real business outcomes.

I’ve led and delivered hiring across:
• Engineering, Product, and Data
• GTM and Commercial teams
• Executive and leadership roles

Working across EMEA, US, LATAM, APAC, and the UAE, I’ve built and scaled teams internationally, often in fast-paced, high-growth environments where hiring speed and quality both matter.

My experience spans:
• End-to-end talent acquisition (hands-on sourcing through to close)
• Building and leading TA functions
• Partnering with founders, CTOs, CHROs, HRBPs, and TA leaders
• Domestic and international expansion hiring
• Implementing data-led and AI-driven hiring strategies

I’m particularly interested in roles where Talent is seen as a true growth lever, not just a support function.

Open to:
• Senior Talent Partner / Talent Business Partner roles
• Talent Lead / Head of Talent opportunities
• High-impact contract or project-based work

If you’re scaling and need someone who can build, deliver, and improve hiring at pace, feel free to reach out or drop me a message.

📩 kcullen1742@gmail.com

leadership brain storming chapter -119

Optimizing hotel labor costs requires a mixed-staffing model, utilizing permanent staff for core operations, alongside seasonal and casual employees to match staffing levels with demand, reducing payroll during slow periods. This strategic mix keeps fixed labor costs low while providing the agility to meet occupancy peaks without overstaffing. 

Key Staffing Types for Cost Management:

# Permanent Employees (Full-Time): Core staff ensuring service quality, consistency, and low turnover costs.

# Seasonal Contract Staffing: Hired for specific peak periods, reducing long-term financial commitments and overheads associated with permanent hiring.

# Casual/Temporary Employees: Ideal for sudden shifts in demand or short-term needs, providing maximum flexibility and reducing overtime costs.

# Cross-trained Staff: Employees trained for multiple roles allow for lean staffing, improving productivity during fluctuations. 

Cost Optimization Strategies:

# Demand-Based Scheduling: Use historical data and AI-powered forecasting to schedule staff, as outlined in.

# Dynamic Staffing: As shown by, adjusting the mix of permanent and casual workers based on daily room occupancy minimizes overstaffing costs.

# Cross-Training: Training existing employees for multiple roles increases flexibility without raising payroll.

# Developing a Talent Pool: Rehire experienced seasonal workers to reduce training costs and maintain high quality.

# Leveraging Technology: Implement software for workforce management and scheduling to optimize efficiency.

🚀 We’re Hiring: AI/ML Engineer – LLM Platform & Model Management (Remote, U.S. Hours)
We’re looking for a skilled AI/ML Engineer with strong experience in LLM platforms, MLOps, and cloud-based ML infrastructure (AWS & GCP) to join our team immediately.

🔍 Role Overview
You’ll work on cutting-edge Generative AI and Large Language Model (LLM) systems, focusing on model deployment, multi-cloud migration, and scalable AI pipelines. This role involves hands-on work with Amazon SageMaker, Google Vertex AI, and LLM APIs.

🚀 We’re Hiring: GenAI Engineer (Copilot / LLM / RAG) – CONTRACT ROLE (Pan India)



We are actively looking for highly skilled professionals with 8+ years of experience in Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning / GenAI to join us on a CONTRACT BASIS. This opportunity is ideal for candidates who have hands-on experience in building enterprise-grade AI Copilots, LLM applications, and Agentic AI systems and are passionate about working on cutting-edge technologies.

🔥 Role Overview

As a GenAI Engineer, you will be responsible for designing, developing, and deploying end-to-end AI-powered applications leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) and modern AI frameworks. You will work on building scalable, production-ready Copilot solutions and intelligent automation systems.

⚡ Key Responsibilities

 ➤ Build and deploy LLM-based GenAI applications end-to-end

 ➤ Work with multiple LLMs such as OpenAI / Azure OpenAI, Gemini, Claude, LLaMA, Mistral.

 ➤ Design and implement Agentic & Multi-Agent systems (MCP / A2A frameworks)

 ➤ Develop robust RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines and document intelligence solutions

 ➤ Leverage frameworks like LangChain, LangGraph, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, Microsoft Copilot

 ➤ Build scalable AI backend services using FastAPI / Flask (Python)

 ➤ Develop and integrate chat interfaces, copilots, dashboards, and APIs

 ➤ Productionize AI solutions and deploy on Azure / AWS / GCP environments

 ➤ Ensure performance optimization, scalability, security, and Responsible AI compliance.

🧠 Required Skills & Experience

 ➤ Strong programming expertise in Python (FastAPI, Flask)

 ➤ Solid understanding of Machine Learning, Deep Learning, NLP, and Generative AI concepts

 ➤ Hands-on experience with LLMs, embeddings, vector search, and prompt engineering

 ➤ Proven experience in designing Agentic AI systems (MCP / A2A)

 ➤ Strong expertise in RAG architectures and vector databases such as FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus.

 ➤ Working knowledge of frontend technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Streamlit / React preferred).

 ➤ Experience with API design and integration (REST / WebSockets)

 ➤ Strong exposure to MLOps & cloud platforms (Azure / AWS / GCP)

 ➤ Experience with Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, MLflow / Azure ML / SageMaker / Vertex AI.

⭐ Good to Have

 ➤ Experience in fine-tuning LLMs (LoRA, PEFT, Hugging Face)

 ➤ Exposure to autonomous agents and async workflows

 ➤ Experience with RPA + Agentic AI integrations

 ➤ Knowledge of AI governance, monitoring, and security frameworks


📌 Important Note

 ⚠️ This is a CONTRACT ROLE (not a permanent/full-time position)

 ⚠️ Candidates should be comfortable working in a contractual engagement

After spending few weeks automating most of Herizon workflows across multiple products, here’s the simplest framework I can give:

Clear inputs + clear outputs + low mistake cost → automate completely. Data processing, content drafts, monitoring, matching.

Repetitive but needs judgment → automate 80%, route the 20% to a human. Support, hiring pipeline, content publishing.

High stakes + context dependent → AI prepares, human decides. Contracts, crises, anything with no undo button.

Most companies are doing one of two things wrong: automating nothing or trying to automate everything. Technically you could automate almost everything hands-off with a openclaw in a box but entire skill is knowing where to put the handoff.

This opportunity is ideal for candidates who have hands-on experience in building enterprise-grade AI Copilots, LLM applications, and Agentic AI systems and are passionate about working on cutting-edge technologies.

🔥 Role Overview
As a GenAI Engineer, you will be responsible for designing, developing, and deploying end-to-end AI-powered applications leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) and modern AI frameworks. You will work on building scalable, production-ready Copilot solutions and intelligent automation systems.

⚡ Key Responsibilities
 ➤ Build and deploy LLM-based GenAI applications end-to-end
 ➤ Work with multiple LLMs such as OpenAI / Azure OpenAI, Gemini, Claude, LLaMA, Mistral.
 ➤ Design and implement Agentic & Multi-Agent systems (MCP / A2A frameworks)
 ➤ Develop robust RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipelines and document intelligence solutions
 ➤ Leverage frameworks like LangChain, LangGraph, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, AutoGen, Google ADK, Microsoft Copilot
 ➤ Build scalable AI backend services using FastAPI / Flask (Python)
 ➤ Develop and integrate chat interfaces, copilots, dashboards, and APIs
 ➤ Productionize AI solutions and deploy on Azure / AWS / GCP environments
 ➤ Ensure performance optimization, scalability, security, and Responsible AI compliance.

🧠 Required Skills & Experience
 ➤ Strong programming expertise in Python (FastAPI, Flask)
 ➤ Solid understanding of Machine Learning, Deep Learning, NLP, and Generative AI concepts
 ➤ Hands-on experience with LLMs, embeddings, vector search, and prompt engineering
 ➤ Proven experience in designing Agentic AI systems (MCP / A2A)
 ➤ Strong expertise in RAG architectures and vector databases such as FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus.
 ➤ Working knowledge of frontend technologies (HTML, CSS, JavaScript, Streamlit / React preferred).
 ➤ Experience with API design and integration (REST / WebSockets)
 ➤ Strong exposure to MLOps & cloud platforms (Azure / AWS / GCP)
 ➤ Experience with Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD pipelines, MLflow / Azure ML / SageMaker / Vertex AI.

⭐ Good to Have
 ➤ Experience in fine-tuning LLMs (LoRA, PEFT, Hugging Face)
 ➤ Exposure to autonomous agents and async workflows
 ➤ Experience with RPA + Agentic AI integrations
 ➤ Knowledge of AI governance, monitoring, and security frameworks

📌 Important Note
 ⚠️ This is a CONTRACT ROLE (not a permanent/full-time position)
 ⚠️ Candidates should be comfortable working in a contractual engagement model.

TCS just cut fresher hiring by 43% — and the real story is bigger than the number.

India’s largest IT company has extended only 25,000 job offers to fresh graduates for FY27 — down sharply from 44,000 in FY26, which was itself one of the highest fresher intakes in the Indian private sector that year.

CEO K Krithivasan confirmed the numbers and was direct: more hiring will happen only when demand clarity improves.

Here’s what’s actually driving this shift:

→ TCS’s total headcount fell by ~24,000 in FY26, even as it hired 44,000 freshers — meaning attrition and layoffs were running parallel to campus hiring
→ 12,000 senior-level roles were cut in FY26, driven by a structural shift in how projects are executed — not AI, as Krithivasan explicitly clarified
→ Freshers need up to 9 months of training before becoming billable; laterals contribute immediately — making fresher hiring a long-term bet that depends on future demand visibility
→ TCS now operates on a demand-driven hiring model, not a talent-pooling one

The broader context: a growing share of entry-level IT tasks — basic coding, testing, debugging — is increasingly being handled by AI tools. Large IT firms are recalibrating what “entry-level” even means in this environment.

For India’s engineering graduates, this is a signal worth reading carefully.

TCS still booked $40 billion in total contract value in FY26. The business is stable. But the old model of mass campus hiring as a default strategy is clearly changing.

What does this mean for the next generation of tech talent in India?

GenZ wants to work remotely

The Millennial wants to work onsite

Hiring Managers want to get “the best person for the job”

And somewhere in the middle… the workplace is quietly having an identity crisis.

Because let’s be honest—this is no longer just about where we work.

It’s about how we define work itself.

A few years ago, the “ideal job” looked predictable:
9–5, physical office, clear hierarchy, slow growth ladder.

Today? That model feels almost… outdated.


Now we have:

👉Remote-first roles that prioritize output over presence

👉Hybrid setups trying (and often struggling) to balance flexibility and control

👉Contract and freelance roles replacing “job security” with “income diversity”

👉Creator-led careers where people build personal brands instead of climbing corporate ladders

👉AI-augmented jobs where your biggest competition isn’t another human—it’s efficiency

So when Gen Z says they want remote work, it’s not just about comfort.

It’s about autonomy. Control. A rejection of rigid systems.

When Millennials push for onsite or hybrid, it’s not outdated thinking.

It’s structure. Collaboration. Stability in a world that’s constantly shifting.

And when hiring managers say they want “the best person for the job,”
what they often mean is:

👉 Someone who can deliver results
👉 Adapt quickly
👉 Communicate clearly
👉 And fit into a system that is still evolving

Here’s the uncomfortable truth most people won’t say out loud:

The job market is no longer designed to make everyone comfortable.

It’s designed to reward:

👉Adaptability over loyalty
👉Skills over tenure
👉Value creation over time spent

So the real question is not:
“Remote or onsite?”
It’s:
👉 Can you thrive in uncertainty?
👉 Can you prove your value regardless of location?
👉 Can you evolve faster than the job market itself?

Because the future of work isn’t one model replacing another.

It’s a messy, unpredictable mix of all of them

And the people who win won’t be the ones arguing about where to work…

They’ll be the ones who understand how to stay relevant, anywhere.

Curious—where do you stand right now? Remote, onsite, or somewhere in between?

The core message is a single, punchy truth that every QA/SDET on LinkedIn feels right now: QA is no longer just testing. AI hasn't replaced quality engineers — it has fundamentally elevated what the job demands. The poster lands that message fast, then backs it with data and trends that give readers something concrete to react to.

The 3 headline stats were chosen deliberately. The 70%+ developer stat (developers routinely rewrite AI-generated code before it's production-ready) is counterintuitive and attention-grabbing — it positions QA as the accountability layer that AI cannot replace. "Self-Healing" and "Agentic" anchor the two biggest tooling shifts happening right now, giving mid-career QA engineers a vocabulary upgrade they can use in their own interviews and LinkedIn conversations.

The 6 trends are grounded in what industry sources — Parasoft, Tricentis, Xray, ACCELQ — are reporting for 2026. They are not generic. Each one maps directly to a skill gap or a hiring signal in the SDET/automation market:
Agentic AI Testing — AI-driven test agents (Tricentis, Testim, etc.) are actively replacing manual trigger-based pipelines. Companies hiring SDET now ask about this in first-round interviews.

Self-Healing Frameworks — Playwright and Cypress are adding AI locator healing natively. This is no longer a premium feature — it's becoming table stakes in 2026 job descriptions.

AI-Code Validation — More than 50% of AI-generated code samples contain logical or security flaws. QA engineers are being asked to build "AI checklists" as a formal process. This is a brand new niche with almost no competition yet.
Shift-Left + Shift-Right — Testing has escaped the QA silo and now runs at every pipeline stage: static analysis at commit, API contract tests at build, real-user observability in production. SDETs who understand this full spectrum command 30–40% higher salaries.

Quality Observability — Production data is feeding back into test coverage decisions. This is the bridge between QA and SRE — a convergence that creates a premium new role profile.

AI Bias & Ethics Testing — As AI systems enter healthcare, finance, and education (your own education platform work is relevant here), QA must now validate that AI doesn't exhibit bias or unsafe behavior. Barely any QA professionals are skilled here yet — massive first-mover advantage.

The shekel hit its strongest in 30 years / USD last week. Will it kill tech exporter margins and threaten jobs in Israel? 

From the Weekend Edition - brought to you by TV10 Israel

Israel continues to attract formidable cross-border capital flow, evidenced this week by Palo Alto Networks’ strategic acquisition of Israeli AI-defender Koi, and EagleNXT’s $10 million equity injection into defense-drone manufacturer ThirdEye Systems. Yet, beneath these headline-grabbing transactions, the day-to-day operational reality for Israeli exporters is deteriorating due to a severe currency mismatch.

Global tech contracts and M&A valuations are universally denominated in US dollars. To understand the sheer scale of this currency drag, look at the recent M&A market. When Google acquired Wiz for $32 billion on March 18, 2025, that capital injection was worth roughly ₪115 billion to the local ecosystem. Today, at a sub-3.00 exchange rate, that exact same $32 billion valuation yields just ₪95 billion. That is a staggering ₪20 billion, a 17.39% contraction, in local purchasing power wiped out by FX mechanics alone.

While repatriated top-line revenue shrinks, operational expenditures remain firmly anchored in the expensive shekel. For US-based multinationals operating massive R&D hubs in Israel, the math is becoming untenable. 

These tech giants fund their global operations in dollars but pay their local workforce in shekels. With the exchange rate dropping below 3.00, the dollar-cost of maintaining those Israeli payrolls just spiked by tens of percentage points. Given that Tel Aviv software engineers are already among the most expensive in the world, this severe currency shock threatens to erase the cost-benefit of hiring locally.

If the USD/ILS pair remains depressed, multinational tech firms and local unicorns may accelerate the offshoring of R&D headcount to cheaper jurisdictions to protect their margins. 

If the threat of multinational hiring freezes or capital flight becomes severe enough, it could be the ultimate catalyst that forces the Bank of Israel’s hand, compelling the central bank to cut interest rates to devalue the shekel and save the tech sector, even at the risk of letting domestic inflation run hot.

Contract vs Perm in Project Services: What’s actually shifting in Melbourne right now? 

We’re seeing a clear reset in how businesses are hiring across transformation and change.

It’s no longer contract vs perm, it’s about when and why.
Right now:
Contract is being used surgically → to deliver critical phases, unblock programs, and bring in niche capability fast 
Perm is being used strategically → to build long-term transformation capability and embed change into the business 

And here’s the shift most aren’t talking about 👇
A lot of organisations are holding perm hiring early, leaning on contract to get moving… then converting once direction is clearer.
Why?
Because transformation itself is changing.
We’re moving from “projects” → to continuous change, heavily influenced by AI, automation, and evolving operating models.
That means:
Shorter, sharper contracts (6–12 months is the norm) 
Greater demand for specialists who can hit the ground running 
And a stronger focus on people who can lead change, not just deliver tasks 

From where I sit, the businesses getting ahead aren’t choosing one model — they’re blending both, deliberately.
Because in this market, flexibility wins.
But clarity on outcomes wins more.
Curious, what are you seeing on the ground?

Give me a call if you want to know what's happening in the Melbourne job market, or simply just send me a DM if you want to connect.

A job interviewer just asked a candidate to use AI mid-interview.

Not to test their coding.
Not to test their AI knowledge.

To see if they could THINK with it.

This happened at McKinsey — the consulting firm that shapes how the world's biggest companies operate.

And it's just the beginning.

Here's the full picture:

→ McKinsey has 25,000 AI agents working alongside 40,000 humans RIGHT NOW
→ 18 months ago? They had just 3,000 agents
→ By end of 2026, AI agents will equal their human headcount
→ Their entire business model is changing — from hourly fees to outcome-based contracts

The CEO said it bluntly:
"When people ask how many people McKinsey employs, I say 60,000 — 40,000 humans and 20,000 agents."

He counts AI as employees.

Not tools. Not software. Employees.

And they're hiring humans who can work WITH those agents — not humans who compete against them.

This changes everything about what a 'good candidate' looks like.

The student who memorised case frameworks?
Might lose to the one who asked better questions to Lilli.

The analyst who worked 80-hour weeks?
Might be replaced by someone who runs 10 agents in parallel and reviews the output.

This is not a warning. It's a signal.

The firms rewriting the rules aren't waiting for regulations or roadmaps.
They're already operating in the new world.

The question is — are you?

Drop your thoughts below. Especially if you're a student or early-career professional — how are you thinking about this? 👇

🚀 AI adoption in Australia isn’t being led by enterprise.
It’s being driven by small businesses - quietly, and much faster than most people realise.

NAB data shows:
🔹 ~42% of small businesses are already using AI
🔹 Another 14% are planning to adopt
🔹 Most common use case: everyday productivity (not transformation)

Let’s be clear: this isn’t “AI strategy.”
No big programs. No steering committees.
This is operators using AI to get immediate leverage in their day-to-day work.
→ Proposals drafted in minutes
→ Contracts summarised instantly
→ Admin work cut down significantly

👉 One small design firm is using AI to review contracts and flag risks.
Not a legal team - just smart use of tools.
👉 A manufacturing business is using AI as a “low-cost strategist” to analyse documents.

That capability didn’t exist for them 12 months ago.

This is what’s actually happening:
AI is becoming a baseline skill.
Not a specialist function. Not a future capability. A baseline.

And this is where the gap is opening up.
Teams that are experimenting → compounding productivity fast
Teams that aren’t → falling behind quietly

From a hiring perspective, most companies are still thinking about this wrong.

It’s not just about hiring AI specialists.

The real demand shift is here:
→ People who can apply AI inside real workflows
→ Operators who can remove 20–40% of manual effort
→ Domain experts who are AI-enabled by default

The uncomfortable question:
How much of your team’s work today is still manual - and doesn’t need to be?
Because that’s where your competitors are already pulling ahead.
Curious - are you seeing real usage in your team, or still in “exploration mode”?

Palantir just posted a manifesto rejecting "regressive cultures" and inclusivity. This isn't just corporate posturing. It's a signal.

For startup founders, this matters more than it looks.

When a major infrastructure vendor makes an ideological declaration, it creates real risks:

1. Talent acquisition challenges
Developers and technical staff increasingly evaluate employers and vendors through a values lens. A vendor flagged for controversial government work (ICE partnerships) may limit your ability to attract diverse, high-quality engineering talent.

2. Regulatory exposure
Companies aligned with specific government agencies face scrutiny that can cascade to their customers. If your core infrastructure provider becomes a political target, your startup inherits that attention.

3. Vendor stability questions
A company explicitly positioning itself against mainstream industry values may face broader market resistance. Scale and funding become unpredictable.

Before choosing any infrastructure vendor in 2026, ask:

- Does this vendor's public stance align with my company's values and hiring goals?
- What government contracts could create downstream liability for me?
- Are there credible alternatives if this vendor becomes politically isolated?

The hidden cost of "cheaper" or "powerful" infrastructure is often the political baggage that comes with it.

#AI #Startups #Infrastructure #TechPolicy #Founders

Understanding labour codes in class is one thing. Seeing how they play out in real life is another.
At Globsyn Business School, while studying human resource management, I’ve been thinking about a simple but uncomfortable question:

Is being “legally compliant” the same as being “ethical”? 🤔

Take recent layoffs across tech companies. Many of them followed the law—notice periods, severance, all checked. ✅

But were they truly planned? Or were they reactions to over-hiring during growth phases and sudden shifts like AI adoption?

In theory, labour codes allow flexibility—contract roles, workforce restructuring, and cost optimisation.

In practice, I’ve seen:
• Teams hired aggressively → then laid off within months
• Roles converted into contractual positions for cost control
• Employees treated as “resources” during growth and “costs” during downturns.

This is not just a legal issue. It’s a management failure in workforce planning and foresight.
As future HR professionals, maybe our role is not just to execute policies but to ask:
👉 Can we balance business needs without making employees feel disposable?
👉 Can we use flexibility without exploiting it?
Because in the end, culture is not built during hiring —
it is revealed during layoffs. ⚖️
#HRM #LabourCodes #FutureOfWork #EthicalLeadership #MBAJourney #HRInsights #WorkforcePlanning #BusinessEthics #GlobsynBusinessSchool

A key takeaway from meetings with clients recently, it’s not always about hiring challenges sometimes it’s about not having enough people leave to even think about a hiring strategy.

On paper, everything can look great:
low attrition, long tenure, really stable teams 🌞🌈

But underneath that:
– engagement lowering
– quite a middle heavy structure
– not a huge amount of movement
– Too comfortable not really evolving teams

It’s something I feel like we’re hearing more and more at the moment this idea of “job hugging.” And it’s only being amplified by the current geopolitical and economic landscape. 

Stability is winning over risk, which makes total sense but it does create a bit of a knock-on effect.
*People stay, but progression slows
* High performers can get a bit stuck
* Businesses end up with less space for new ideas or different skill sets 

How can you future proof with a team thats living in the BAU work and is no longer innovating I guess?

This is a pretty big shift from what we were hearing even 12–18 months ago.
From a hiring perspective, it’s also interesting:
* Perm hiring is more considered
* Experience requirements are higher AI literacy being a new big one!
* We’re seeing more contract roles used to bring in fresh thinking without adding long term headcount

Feels like we’re moving from a retention problem to more of a stagnation one.

I as Principal at Zed Law am featured in an article today discussing how AI is reshaping employment law in Australia, and why most workplace contracts aren't keeping up.

This is something we see every day at Zed Law. Employers are rolling out AI across hiring, performance reviews, and workflow automation, but their employment agreements are still written for a world of laptops and email.

Here's what I had to say:

"Most employment agreements in Australia were drafted for a world where 'technology' meant a company laptop and email. Now employers are deploying AI across hiring, performance monitoring and workflow automation, but the contractual and regulatory framework hasn't caught up."

"We're seeing employers expose themselves to risk because their employment agreements don't contemplate AI‑driven surveillance, algorithmic decision-making, or the IP implications of employees using tools like ChatGPT on the job."

The more employers lean on AI to make or inform decisions, from recruitment to discipline to redundancy, the more critical it becomes to update contracts, policies and notices.

If your employment agreements haven't been reviewed with AI in mind, now is the time.

New Edition Alert: The $70.8 Million Shortcut: When Buying Customers Beats Building Them (Wipro - Day 1 of 3)

Wipro just paid $70.8 million to acquire select customer contracts and engineering teams from Alpha Net Consulting, not a competitor, not a product, but a portfolio of relationships and delivery capacity. In an AI-first services market where speed-to-relevance is the new currency, this is a bet that inorganic growth can close capability gaps faster than organic hiring ever could.

The Unresolved Tension - Wipro’s margins are under pressure, operating margins dropped to 17.3% in Q4, with management citing the transition to higher-margin AI consulting as a reason. The tension is this: acquisitions add revenue but also add integration costs. The Alpha Net deal is small enough to digest, but the cumulative integration of three major acquisitions in nine months will test Wipro’s PMO capacity. The unresolved question is whether the “services-as-a-software” model can deliver the margin expansion that justifies the acquisition spend or whether Wipro is buying its way into a lower-margin future.

Reflecting on over 10 years as a sales professional in the South East Asia energy sector, I have seen firsthand how the technical sales landscape has evolved. Navigating this region requires more than just a strong product; it requires a deep understanding of the unique intersection between high stakes engineering and relationship-driven business cultures.
Here are the core pillars that define success in the South East Asia market today:

1. Relationship Driven Partnerships
Success is built on long term trust and reliability. Especially when working with National Oil Companies (NOCs), we act as technical consultants rather than just vendors, solving specific operational challenges through deep collaboration.

2. Strategic Local Compliance
Local content requirements (in markets like Malaysia and Indonesia) are central to the value proposition. Integrating local partnerships, hiring and procurement into your strategy is essential for navigating these fragmented regulatory landscapes.

3. Value Based Solutions
In a volatile market, the focus has shifted from initial cost to Total Cost of Ownership (TCO). Whether implementing all electric infrastructure or HPHT technologies, the goal is to demonstrate long term ROI and operational efficiency in demanding environments.

4. The Digital and Sustainable Evolution
The industry is rapidly adopting AI driven diagnostics, digital twins and low carbon solutions. Moving towards integrated service contracts allow us to align our goals directly with the operator’s long term performance and sustainability targets.

South East Asia remains one of the most diverse and technically challenging markets in the world. Success here is a marathon, not a sprint, grounded in local expertise and a commitment to innovation.

#OilFieldServices #TechnicalSales

Software engineering hiring in the US is evolving — not disappearing.
While headlines often focus on layoffs, the broader data tells a more nuanced story.
📊 Current market trends show:
• Demand remains strong for senior engineers with real production experience
 • Backend, AI, cloud, cybersecurity, and data engineering roles continue to lead openings
 • Companies are hiring leaner teams, expecting broader ownership and stronger business impact
 • Mid-level generic profiles face more competition than specialists
 • Contract and remote opportunities remain significant, especially for global talent
What changed is not the need for engineers — it’s the profile companies want.
In many cases, one engineer today is expected to combine:
✅ Software engineering fundamentals
 ✅ Cloud / DevOps awareness
 ✅ Product thinking
 ✅ AI tooling leverage
 ✅ Communication and ownership
The market is rewarding adaptability more than ever.
Engineers who can build systems, understand business context, and move fast with modern tools are still highly valuable.
My view:
The era of “just coding tickets” is fading.
The era of engineers who solve business problems end-to-end is accelerating.
For those in the market now, the question is less “Are there jobs?” and more:
👉 “Does my profile match where demand is moving?”

2020: Middle East imported Chinese medical equipment.

2026: Middle East co-develops AI healthcare infrastructure with China.

Six years. Complete transformation.

1. Why Now: The Timing Unlocked

Saudi Vision 2030: $65B healthcare investment

· Target: 290 hospitals and 2,300 primary care centers privatized
· NEOM mandate: AI solutions must comprise 30%+ of infrastructure

UAE HELM Cluster: $25.6B GDP target by 2045

· $42B capital inflow expected
· Focus: Biotech + pharma manufacturing + AI diagnostics

Middle East AI oncology market alone: $112M (2024) → $813M (2034)

These aren't pilot budgets. These are nation-building budgets.

And they require partners who can build nations, not just sell products.

2. The Value Exchange: What Each Side Brings
 
What China Offers:

· Recent national-scale deployment experience
· Full-stack capabilities (hardware + software + training + maintenance)
· Willingness to commit long-term (facilities, local teams, tech transfer)
· Speed (18-month deployment)

What Middle East Offers:

· Sovereign capital backing
· Greenfield opportunity (building new vs. retrofitting legacy systems)
· Regional gateway (GCC success → broader MENA market access)
· Government partnership legitimacy

Recent examples:

BGI's "Genalive" JV → RMB 950M contract

XtalPi's UAE lab → First automated R&D facility

SynYi's Saudi pilot → World's first AI clinic

Not vendor-buyer. Co-development.

3. What it Actually Takes

The Requirements:

Cultural Integration:

· Religious considerations embedded in product design
· Gender-segregated care pathways where required
· Arabic language support non-negotiable

Operational Commitment:

· Local facilities
· Local hiring and training programs
· Multi-year presence

Data Sovereignty:

· Absolute local hosting and control
· Full audit trail and compliance
· No data leaves the country

The Timeline:

Year 1: Relationship building, compliance setup, pilot approval

Year 2-3: Deployment, refinement, expansion discussions

Year 4+: Scale, regional replication, sustained operations

This isn't a quick win.

It's infrastructure partnership thinking.

4. The Opportunity Middle East Represents

For Chinese healthcare AI companies:

· Build international reference cases with sovereign validation
· Access patient capital for long-term R&D
· Gateway to broader MENA region

For Middle East:

· Accelerate healthcare transformation without legacy constraints
· Build local AI capabilities and talent pool
· Position as regional healthtech hub

When both sides win structurally, partnerships last.

The partnership isn't hypothetical anymore.

It's operational, growing, and reshaping both ecosystems.

P.S. What makes a healthcare AI partnership successful long-term?

Tamara Sunbul, MD, MBA, FHIMSS, CPHIMS, PMP | Rizwan Tufail | Pankaj Bajaj PMP, MBA | Dr. Sergio Sicbaldi | Albarah Elkhani MSc, BSc CSSBB | Mohaymen Abdelghany

Enterprise AI doesn't have a model problem. It has a library problem.

Imagine a vast library; millions of documents, reports, contracts, customer records, financial filings. You hire the smartest librarian in the world. But the books have no index. No system. No way to find what is relevant to the question being asked.

The librarian fails. Not because they aren't smart enough. Because the library wasn't built for them to succeed.

This is the reality of most enterprise AI deployments today. Companies are hiring world class models and dropping them into a library with no retrieval system. Then wondering why the answers are wrong or hallucinated.

RAG: Retrieval Augmented Generation, solves this. It's not about making the librarian smarter. It's about building the system that lets any librarian walk in and instantly find the right book.

This is where companies like Pinecone and Weaviate come in. They build vector databases — the indexing infrastructure that makes enterprise data findable by AI in real time. Frameworks like LangChain and LlamaIndex sit on top, connecting the model to the right sources at the right moment.

Unglamorous work. Invisible to most people. Strategically everything.

Here's what most people miss. The model is replaceable. A better one comes out every quarter. But the retrieval infrastructure, the indexing, the connectors, the data pipelines built around your specific enterprise data, that's sticky. That's where the real switching cost lives.

Whoever owns the data pipeline owns the enterprise relationship.
Not the model provider. Not the cloud. The company that makes the library work.

I've spent 22 years watching this pattern play out across three platform cycles, Nokia in mobile services, Samsung in gaming, Apple in developer ecosystems. The platforms that won weren't always the ones with the best technology. They were the ones that made it easiest to build on top of them. The SDK. The connector. The integration layer. That's where lock-in happened every single time.

Enterprise AI is running the same playbook. The race to own the retrieval layer is on. And it's still wide open.

The AI skill gap isn't a hiring problem. It's a delivery problem.

Everyone's talking about hiring AI talent. The stats are dramatic — 72% of employers globally can't find the skills they need. AI literacy is now harder to source than traditional IT.

But here's what nobody's saying:
Most Microsoft consultancies/partners don't need to hire AI specialists. They need to deliver with them.
The real gap isn't in your recruitment pipeline. It's in the project that just kicked off with a Copilot integration your team hasn't done before. It's the Dynamics 365 engagement where the client suddenly wants AI-driven workflows and your bench doesn't have that depth.
Hiring takes months. Your SOW started last Monday.

Peter Sugar brings a very specific—and rare—blend of clearance-focused recruiting expertise, technical fluency, and consistent delivery at scale. In a market where cleared talent is scarce and timelines are unforgiving, that combination is what makes him stand out.
At a high level, his value comes from operating at the intersection of mission-critical hiring and hard-to-access talent pools. Recruiting for Secret-TS/SCI and polygraph-cleared roles isn’t just “harder recruiting”—it’s a completely different game. Peter already understands the clearance landscape, LCAT constraints, program urgency, and compliance requirements, which means he can contribute immediately without a learning curve.
What really differentiates him is how he translates that knowledge into results:
1. Proven ability to deliver in constrained talent markets
Placing 300+ cleared professionals—especially at the Secret-TS/SCI and Full Scope Poly level—signals deep access to networks most recruiters never reach. This isn’t just sourcing; it’s long-term relationship building within highly vetted communities.
2. Consistent, high-volume output without sacrificing quality
750 hires overall and a steady 3–4 hires per month across concurrent programs shows reliability. Many recruiters can spike performance temporarily; fewer can sustain it across multiple contracts with different requirements.
3. Speed as a competitive advantage
A 30% reduction in time-to-fill is meaningful in GovCon, where delays can cost contracts or impact mission delivery. This suggests he’s not just filling roles—he’s optimizing the hiring process itself through better sourcing strategies and screening efficiency.
4. Strong technical recruiting depth
He’s not limited to one niche. Covering software engineering, DevOps, cloud, cybersecurity, and AI/ML means he can support full program builds, not just isolated roles. That versatility is especially valuable for primes and subs scaling entire delivery teams.
5. Full-cycle ownership and stakeholder alignment
His experience working closely with hiring managers and program leaders indicates he’s not just a pipeline generator—he’s embedded in the business. That includes managing expectations, navigating clearance nuances, and closing candidates in competitive situations.
6. GovCon fluency and program awareness
Understanding contract vehicles, LCATs, and agency-specific dynamics is a major differentiator. It reduces friction, improves submission quality, and increases the likelihood of hires sticking and meeting compliance standards.
